questions.question_text: A brokerage's social media advertising comes under IDFPR scrutiny. Under Illinois rules, who has the duty to supervise all advertising, in any medium, of the brokerage's licensed services?
distractors: The brokerage's marketing department
IDFPR as the licensing authority
The local Realtor association
questions.explanation: Under 68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.705(b)(5), the designated managing broker's supervisory duties include supervising all advertising, in any medium, of any service requiring a license. Pre-publication review and approval is specifically required only for advertising by new brokers who have not completed the 45-hour post-license education (225 ILCS 454/10-55(b)). A managing broker who fails to properly supervise is subject to discipline.

questions.question_text: Which document must a managing broker maintain to satisfy Illinois supervisory requirements for the brokerage?
distractors: A notarized supervisory oath filed with IDFPR
Quarterly activity reports for each broker
Individual performance evaluation records
questions.explanation: Illinois requires managing brokers to maintain written office policies and procedures covering the activities of all sponsored licensees. This written documentation is a core supervisory obligation under the License Act.

questions.question_text: A managing broker fails to provide oversight of a sponsored broker's trust fund handling, resulting in client losses. What is the most likely regulatory consequence for the managing broker?
distractors: A warning letter with no further action
Automatic license renewal denial only
Liability limited to the sponsored broker alone
questions.explanation: Negligent supervision is grounds for discipline against the managing broker's license. The managing broker must ensure proper trust fund handling by all sponsored licensees and can face sanctions when that duty is neglected.

questions.question_text: A newly sponsored broker asks what training the managing broker must provide. Which statement best describes the Illinois requirement?
questions.correct_answer: Adequate training from the managing broker
distractors: Training is optional after initial licensure
Only continuing education courses are required
A state-approved program assigned by IDFPR
questions.explanation: Illinois law requires the managing broker to provide adequate training to sponsored brokers. This is part of the managing broker's overall supervisory responsibility for all licensed activities conducted under their oversight.

questions.question_text: A managing broker discovers a sponsored broker has been depositing earnest money into a personal account. Beyond correcting the behavior, what supervisory failure does this expose?
questions.correct_answer: Failure to ensure proper trust fund handling
distractors: Failure to collect a sufficient earnest money deposit
Failure to register the broker with IDFPR
Failure to maintain branch office signage
questions.explanation: Earnest money must be deposited into a trust or escrow account, never a personal account. The managing broker is responsible for ensuring that all sponsored brokers follow proper trust fund procedures, and this situation reveals a supervisory lapse.

questions.question_text: An Illinois brokerage opens a new branch office. What staffing requirement must the brokerage satisfy at that location?
questions.correct_answer: A designated managing broker named for that office
distractors: Any licensed broker without formal designation
Automatic coverage by the main office managing broker
An unlicensed office administrator approved by IDFPR
questions.explanation: Under 225 ILCS 454/5-45(b), the sponsoring broker must name a designated managing broker for each office and report all designated managing brokers, and the office or offices they manage, to IDFPR (changes within 15 days). The same individual may be designated for more than one office, but the designation must be made and reported; branch supervision is never automatic. Illinois has no 'assistant managing broker' designation.

questions.question_text: A broker passes the Illinois licensing exam but has not yet obtained sponsorship from a managing broker. Can this individual practice real estate?
questions.correct_answer: No, a sponsoring managing broker is required first
distractors: Yes, for up to 90 days while seeking sponsorship
Yes, if the broker notifies IDFPR of intent to practice
Yes, but only for transactions under $50,000
questions.explanation: In Illinois, a broker cannot practice real estate without a sponsoring managing broker. The sponsorship must be registered with IDFPR before any licensed activity can take place.

questions.question_text: During an IDFPR inspection, an investigator asks to see the licenses of all brokers working at the office. What display standard must the brokerage meet?
distractors: Only the managing broker's license must be visible
Kept solely in IDFPR's online database
Mailed to IDFPR before each renewal period
questions.explanation: Under 68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.730(a), the license or a copy of the license of every sponsored licensee must be readily available to the public at the sponsoring broker's office or through electronic means provided by the sponsoring broker. Each licensee also carries their license, or an electronic version, on their person (225 ILCS 454/5-50(e)).

questions.question_text: A sponsored broker changes brokerage firms. What must happen before the broker can begin working at the new firm?
questions.correct_answer: New sponsorship must be registered with IDFPR
distractors: The broker must wait 30 days between firms
The previous managing broker must approve the transfer
The broker must retake the state licensing exam
questions.explanation: When a broker changes firms, the new sponsorship must be registered with IDFPR before the broker can engage in licensed activity under the new managing broker. There is no mandatory waiting period between sponsorships.

questions.question_text: An Illinois sponsoring broker operates a main office and two branch offices. How must the brokerage license be handled?
distractors: Kept only at the principal office
Carried on the managing broker's person at all times
Filed with the county recorder where each office is located
questions.explanation: Under 225 ILCS 454/5-45(a), the brokerage license must be displayed conspicuously in each office. Individual licensees, by contrast, carry their own license or an electronic version on their person (225 ILCS 454/5-50(e)), and a copy of every sponsored licensee's license must be readily available to the public at the office or through electronic means (68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.730).

questions.question_text: Under Illinois law effective January 1, 2025, when must a brokerage agreement be in writing?
questions.correct_answer: For all agreements regardless of exclusivity
distractors: Only for exclusive right-to-sell listing contracts
Only when the agreement exceeds six months
Only for commercial property transactions
questions.explanation: As of January 1, 2025, Illinois requires all brokerage agreements to be in writing, whether exclusive or non-exclusive. This change expanded the written agreement requirement to cover every type of brokerage engagement.

questions.question_text: A seller asks a broker what the standard commission rate is in Illinois. How should the broker respond?
distractors: The rate is set by the local Realtor association
State law establishes a maximum commission rate
IDFPR publishes recommended rate schedules
questions.explanation: Commission rates in Illinois are never set by law or trade association. Compensation is always negotiable between the broker and the client. Suggesting otherwise would be a violation of antitrust principles.

questions.question_text: A buyer's broker receives a cooperating commission from the listing broker. Does this payment establish an agency relationship between the buyer's broker and the seller?
questions.correct_answer: No, commission source does not determine agency
distractors: Yes, accepting payment creates a dual agency relationship
Yes, the broker now represents both parties
Only if the payment exceeds a statutory threshold
questions.explanation: In Illinois, the source of commission payment does not establish or define an agency relationship. A buyer's broker can be compensated by the listing broker or the seller and still represent only the buyer.

questions.question_text: A broker contacts a seller who already has an active exclusive listing with another brokerage and attempts to solicit the listing. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Interference with an existing listing contract
distractors: Practicing without a valid sponsorship agreement
Failure to disclose agency to the seller
Operating a branch office without designation
questions.explanation: Illinois law prohibits brokers from interfering with existing contracts or listings held by another brokerage. Actively soliciting a seller who is under an exclusive listing agreement with another firm is a clear violation of this rule.

questions.question_text: A listing broker in Illinois is compensated by the buyer rather than the seller in a transaction. Is this arrangement permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, a broker can be paid by any party
distractors: No, the listing broker must be paid by the seller
Only if IDFPR grants a written exception
Only in commercial transactions over $1 million
questions.explanation: Illinois allows a broker to be compensated by a party they do not represent. The source of compensation does not determine the agency relationship, so a listing broker may lawfully accept payment from the buyer if agreed upon.
